story: Boom! The sky lit up as American soldiers in Normandy, France, celebrated July 4 with a thunderous display. title: A Magical Tale Born on a Boat
story: Splash! A boat glided along the River Isis in Oxford. Inside sat Charles Dodgson, telling a fantastical story to young Alice Liddell. Little did they know, on July 4, 1862, literary magic was happening. Dodgson's tale of a curious girl tumbling down a rabbit hole would become the beloved classic 'Alice in Wonderland.' Writing as Lewis Carroll, he'd bring wonder to generations of readers.
activity_question: What was the name of the river where the story of Alice in Wonderland was first told?
bonus_fact: The River Isis is actually part of the River Thames as it flows through Oxford.

title: The Sky's Biggest Boom!
story: Kaboom! The night sky lit up like never before. Chinese astronomers spotted an incredible sight on July 4, 1054 - the brightest supernova ever seen! This cosmic explosion was so dazzling, it could be seen during the day for weeks. The stellar blast left behind a beautiful cloud of gas and dust we now call the Crab Nebula. It's still visible today with telescopes, a cosmic reminder of that long-ago stellar fireworks show.

title: The Year with an Extra Day
story: Clocks went wild in Western Samoa in 1892! The country decided to switch sides of the International Date Line, causing a time-bending twist. This change meant Western Samoa experienced 367 days that year instead of the usual 365. Even stranger, July 4 happened twice, with two Mondays in a row. Imagine celebrating Independence Day two days in a row!
activity_question: What unusual event happened in Western Samoa in 1892?
activity_choices: They had two New Year's Eves
follow_up_question: How do you think people felt about having an extra day in the year?

title: Soccer Surprise: West Germany's Stunning Victory
story: Helmut Rahn became a soccer hero in 1954. On July 4, he scored two goals in a thrilling match. West Germany faced Hungary in the FIFA World Cup Final at Wankdorf Stadium in Bern, Switzerland. title: A Patriotic Song Debuts
story: Crowds gathered at Park Street Church in Boston for a special treat. Samuel Francis Smith had penned new lyrics to a familiar tune. As voices joined in harmony, 'America (My Country 'Tis of Thee)' rang out for the first time in public on July 4, 1831. The patriotic song quickly captured hearts, becoming a beloved national hymn.
activity_question: Where did 'America (My Country 'Tis of Thee)' have its first public performance?
follow_up_question: Who wrote the lyrics to this patriotic song?

title: Fishy Fun in San Francisco
story: Splash! The first public aquarium in San Francisco made waves when it opened in Woodward Gardens in 1873. Visitors marveled at colorful fish swimming in glass tanks, bringing the wonders of the ocean to the city. This July 4th attraction gave people a chance to see underwater life up close, sparking curiosity about marine creatures.
activity_question: Where did San Francisco's first public aquarium open?
follow_up_question: What kinds of sea creatures do you think people saw at this new aquarium?

title: A Song for America's Beauty
story: Inspired by a breathtaking view from Pikes Peak, Katherine Lee Bates penned a poem that would become an iconic American anthem. In 1895, on July 4, she published 'America the Beautiful' in a church newspaper. The stirring words celebrated the nation's natural wonders and hope for a bright future. Soon set to music, it became a beloved patriotic song sung across the country.
activity_question: What inspired Katherine Lee Bates to write 'America the Beautiful'?
activity_choices: A breathtaking view from Pikes Peak
A visit to the Statue of Liberty
A trip to the Grand Canyon
activity_answer: A breathtaking view from Pikes Peak
follow_up_question: Can you name another famous patriotic song about America?

title: Vermont's Hottest Day Ever
story: Whew! It was sizzling in Vermont on July 4, 1911. The tiny town of Vernon felt like it was melting as temperatures soared to a scorching 105°F (41°C). This wasn't just hot; it was the hottest day ever recorded in Vermont's history! Imagine trying to enjoy a holiday picnic or parade in that sweltering heat.
activity_question: What was the record-breaking temperature in Vernon, Vermont?
follow_up_question: How do you think people stayed cool on such a hot day before air conditioning?
